answer:
Across the smriti corpus, 'ńĀŇõrama' denotes a life-stage or order of life. The classical four ńĀŇõramas are: (1) brahmacńĀri (celibate student), (2) gŠĻõhastha (householder), (3) vńĀnaprastha (forest-dweller/hermit), and (4) saŠĻÉnyńĀsi/yati (ascetic/renunciant). The major smritis all recognize these four stages, but they do not treat them identically.

**ManusmŠĻõti** (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, ch. 6) gives the most systematic ńĀŇõrama doctrine. At Mn_6.87, it states: 'The celibate student, the householder, the forest-dweller, and the ascetic‚ÄĒthese four separate ńĀŇõramas originate from the householder' (p-59814f). The text insists on sequential, orderly progression (Mn_6.88), and explicitly elevates the householder above all others: 'the householder is declared to be the most excellent, for he supports these other three' (Mn_6.89, p-2acddc), using the simile that all rivers find rest in the ocean as all ńĀŇõramas find support in the householder (Mn_6.90, p-a6e55e). One must discharge three debts (Vedic study, sons, sacrifice) before legitimately seeking liberation (Mn_6.35‚Äď37). Chapter 6 devotes extensive coverage to the forest-dweller and ascetic stages. Manu also notes differential ritual intensity across ńĀŇõramas (e.g., purification is double for celibate students, triple for forest-dwellers, quadruple for ascetics ‚ÄĒ Mn_5.137).

**YńĀj√Īavalkya SmŠĻõti** (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6) covers the four ńĀŇõramas across two chapters: student and householder in ch. 1 (sections 2 and 5), forest-dweller (vńĀnaprasthadharmaprakaraŠĻáam) and ascetic (yatidharmaprakaraŠĻáam) in ch. 3 (sections 3‚Äď4). Notably, Yj_3.65 (p-f263eb) qualifies the ńĀŇõrama system: 'The stage of life is not the cause of dharma; it becomes so only when it is practiced. Therefore, what is harmful to oneself, one should not do to others.' This is a more flexible and internalized stance than Manu's ‚ÄĒ the external ńĀŇõrama identity is not itself the source of dharma, but the actual practice of its principles is.

**ViŠĻ£ŠĻáu SmŠĻõti** (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8) frames its entire text as declaring 'the eternal laws of the four varŠĻáas along with the practices of the ńĀŇõramas' (Vi_1.62‚Äď63, p-cdc451). Its structural distribution is telling: the brahmacńĀri occupies ch. 28, the householder spans chs. 58‚Äď93 (dozens of chapters), the vńĀnaprastha gets chs. 94‚Äď95 (brief), and the renunciant (pravrajyńĀŇõramńę) gets ch. 96. The transition to the forest is triggered by visible ageing or grandchildren (Vi_94.1‚Äď2), and renunciation (Vi_96.1) requires 'having been purified of his passions in the three stages of life' ‚ÄĒ a sequential prerequisite echoing Manu.

**ParńĀŇõara SmŠĻõti** (unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be) positions itself as specifically for the Kali Yuga (Par_1.24), focusing on simplified householder conduct and hospitality duties. It does not develop a systematic four-ńĀŇõrama doctrine.

**Other smritis** in the corpus (ńÄŠĻÖgirasa, NńĀrada, BŠĻõhaspati, KńĀtyńĀyana) deal primarily with ritual impurity, ŇõrńĀddha, and legal/vyavahńĀra matters; they mention ascetics and householders in scattered rules but do not expound the four-ńĀŇõrama system as such.

**Summary of variation**: The four ńĀŇõramas are universally acknowledged across the corpus, but they are not treated identically. Manusmriti gives the most elaborate sequential doctrine, explicitly crowning the householder as supreme. YńĀj√Īavalkya's SmŠĻõti introduces a notable qualification that the ńĀŇõrama label alone does not confer dharmic status ‚ÄĒ practice matters more than stage. ViŠĻ£ŠĻáu SmŠĻõti shares Manu's sequential logic but reveals different structural priorities by devoting far more space to householder duties. ParńĀŇõara SmŠĻõti largely bypasses the ńĀŇõrama framework, offering Kali-yuga-specific conduct instead.
